::: {.cell}

:::



# The age old question, "cats or dogs", has long been debated. From ancient times, both have been revered as companions, utilized for their hunting prowess, and appreciated for their beauty. But...... how do we feel about them at EPA?

## Counts and Shares



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Votes and shares for Cats vs Dogs.](index_files/figure-html/fig-bar-1.png){#fig-bar width=576}
:::
:::



Dogs share: 66.7%, Cats share: 33.3%.
Vote margin (Dogs − Cats): 14 votes = 33.3%.

## Statistical difference from 50/50

Testing Cats proportion p against 0.5 using an exact binomial test.



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Exact binomial test

data:  14 and N
number of successes = 14, number of trials = 42, p-value = 0.04356
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.1956680 0.4954875
sample estimates:
probability of success 
             0.3333333 
```


:::
:::



Two‑sided p‑value: 0.0436
95% exact CI for p(Cats): 0.196 to 0.495

Express the Dogs lead as a margin in share terms, margin = p(Dogs) − p(Cats) = 1 − 2p(Cats). The corresponding 95% CI:



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.608663904 0.009025071
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95
```


:::
:::



Even the lower bound of the margin is positive, indicating a clear preference for Dogs.

How many flips would change the winner?

To tie, you’d need 7 votes to flip (7 Dogs → Cats).
To give Cats a lead, you’d need 8 flips (8 votes).

## How unusual is 14-28 under a fair 50/50 world?

Under p = 0.5 with N = 42, the probability to be at least this far from 50/50 (two‑sided) is the binomial test p‑value.

We can also simulate the Cats counts under 50/50:



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Simulated Cats counts under a 50/50 world (10,000 polls, N = 42). The observed 14 is marked.](index_files/figure-html/fig-sim-null-1.png){#fig-sim-null width=672}
:::
:::



Fraction of simulations at least as extreme (two‑sided):



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.045
```


:::
:::



## Likelihood and Bayesian view for the Cats share

Binomial likelihood for p(Cats) given 14 out of 42 votes:



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Binomial likelihood L(p) for p(Cats) given 14/42 (scaled to max = 1).](index_files/figure-html/fig-likelihood-1.png){#fig-likelihood width=576}
:::
:::



With a flat prior Beta(1,1), the posterior is Beta(15,29). Credible interval and the probability Cats < 0.5 (i.e., Dogs lead):



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Posterior for p(Cats) with Beta(1,1) prior (posterior = Beta(15,29)).](index_files/figure-html/fig-posterior-1.png){#fig-posterior width=576}
:::
:::



95% credible interval for p(Cats): [0.210, 0.485]

Posterior probability Dogs lead (p(Cats) < 0.5): 0.9842

Posterior predictive for a repeat poll of size 42: chance Cats win, tie, or Dogs win.



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
pp_cat_win     pp_tie pp_dog_win 
   0.05165    0.02770    0.92065 
```


:::
:::



Bayes factor for “not 50/50” vs “p = 0.5” using Jeffreys prior Beta(0.5,0.5).



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1.319843
```


:::
:::



BF10 > 1 supports “not 50/50”; values around 3–10 are often called “moderate” evidence.

## Effect sizes and ratios



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
risk_difference      risk_ratio       odds_dogs        cohens_h 
      0.3333333       2.0000000       2.0000000       0.6796738 
```


:::
:::



Dogs lead by 33.3% in share.
Dogs have about 2:1 the share of Cats and odds about 2:1.
Cohen’s h ≈ 0.68 (medium‑to‑large difference; 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large).

## Waffle chart (one square per voter)



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Waffle chart: 42 voters, Cats = 14, Dogs = 28.](index_files/figure-html/fig-waffle-1.png){#fig-waffle width=576}
:::
:::



# Takeaways

Dogs win clearly: 28–14 yields a Dogs margin of 33.3%.

Exact binomial test vs 50/50 is significant (p ≈ 0.0436), and the 95% CI for p(Cats) lies below 0.5.

A fair coin process would rarely produce a result this lopsided for N = 42.

Bayesian analysis strongly favors a Dogs lead and predicts Dogs would likely win a same‑size repeat poll.

To tie you’d need 7 flips; to flip the winner, 8 flips.

# Unfortunately, its a resounding vote for dogs. Cats, better luck next time.
